required.title: [Independent Reading] Chapter books of genuine literary quality
required.author: C.S. Lewis (if not read in A1), *The Hobbit* by J.R.R. Tolkien, *Mrs. Frisby and the Rats of NIMH* by Robert C. O'Brien, *The Cricket in Times Square* by George Selden, *My Side of the Mountain* by Jean Craighead George, *Island of the Blue Dolphins* by Scott O'Dell.

required.title: [Read Aloud / Independent] Folk tale collections from world traditions
required.author: Virginia Hamilton (African American folk tales), *The Jataka Tales* (Buddhist teaching stories), *Arabian Nights* (age-appropriate selections), Greek and Norse mythology collections, Native American folk tale collections.

required.title: [Read Aloud / Independent] Mythology
required.author: Mary Pope Osborne, or other age-appropriate mythology collections.

competence_markers.description: The child reads independently for at least 20-30 minutes daily and can narrate what they read with detail and interpretation
The child has read at least two complete chapter books independently during this packet
The child can compare stories from different cultural traditions, identifying both similarities and differences in what each tradition values
The child can retell and compare at least two creation stories from different cultures
The child participates in literary conversations that go beyond plot summary — discussing meaning, values, and personal responses
The child can express disagreement with a story's worldview and explain why, showing the beginnings of critical engagement
The child has read folk tales from at least five cultural traditions and can name stories from traditions other than their own
The child shows sustained engagement with independent reading — choosing to read, asking for new books, discussing what they have read with enthusiasm

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about the book you are reading right now.
narration_prompts.detail: What is happening? Who are the main characters? What do they want?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a folk tale we read this week.
narration_prompts.detail: What happened? What did the story think was important?

narration_prompts.prompt: Compare two stories from different cultures that are about similar things.
narration_prompts.detail: How were they alike? How were they different? What did each culture seem to value?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a creation story we read.
narration_prompts.detail: How did that culture say the world began? What does that tell you about what they believed?

narration_prompts.prompt: Did you read a story this week that you disagreed with?
narration_prompts.detail: What did the story want you to think, and why did you disagree?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the most important thing stories have taught you so far?

cross_connections.description: While 3.2.5-A2 develops worldview awareness through independent reading, 3.2.4-A builds the literary experience foundation through read-alouds and memorization. The two packets are complementary: one builds the habit of noticing what stories mean; the other builds the habit of engaging with literary quality.

The creation stories, myths, and folk tales compared here are the same material studied in 1.3.1 as expressions of worldview. The overlap is intentional — encountering the same stories through literature and through worldview study deepens understanding of both.

The myths and folk tales encountered here are products of the civilizations studied in 1.1.1. Greek myths belong to ancient Greece. Norse myths belong to the Viking world. The student who reads them alongside civilizational history sees stories as part of a living culture.
The child who has read widely across folk tale and mythological traditions has a rich vocabulary of story patterns, character types, and narrative structures for their own creative work in 3.2.3.

Different cultures' stories embody different visions of virtue. The comparison of folk tales and myths across traditions in this packet directly supports the cross-cultural virtue awareness developed in IC1.2.

content.why_this_matters:
You have been listening to stories read aloud for years. You have learned to ask what stories want you to think. Now you are going to read stories on your own — chapter books, folk tales, myths, and longer narratives that take days or weeks to finish. And you are going to get better at the most important skill in this sub-domain: seeing the world that a story creates and understanding what that world assumes about how life works.

Independent reading changes something fundamental about your relationship with stories. When someone reads to you, you experience the story through their voice, their pacing, their emphasis. When you read on your own, the story speaks directly to your mind. You set the pace. You decide when to pause and think. You inhabit the world of the story in a way that is entirely yours. This is one of the most powerful experiences a human being can have — entering another world through words on a page — and it becomes possible only when you can read fluently enough to stop thinking about the words themselves and start living inside the story.

At the same time, you will continue to talk about what you read — with your mentor, with your family, with other readers. Literary conversation is not a quiz about the book. It is a way of thinking together about what the story meant, what it assumed, and whether you agree. The child who reads a story and then discusses it with another thoughtful person understands the story more deeply than the child who reads alone in silence. Stories are meant to be shared.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Independent reading habit** — the child reads on their own daily, building the practice that sustains a lifelong reading life
2. **Sustained narrative comprehension** — the child can follow a story across chapters and days, tracking characters, events, and themes
3. **Narration after independent reading** — the child retells and discusses what they have read, demonstrating comprehension and interpretation
4. **Worldview awareness through folk tales and mythology** — the child encounters the foundational stories of multiple civilizations and notices what each tradition values
5. **Literary conversation** — the child participates in discussions about books that go beyond plot summary to meaning and interpretation
6. **The bridge from listener to reader** — the child transitions from passive recipient of stories to active, independent reader
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Reading on Your Own

When you read a book on your own, something different happens in your mind compared to when someone reads to you. You hear the characters' voices in your own head. You picture the places. You set the speed — you can race through an exciting part or slow down when something is confusing or beautiful. The story becomes yours in a way it cannot when someone else is telling it to you.

Independent reading is a skill that grows with practice. The more you read, the easier it gets. The easier it gets, the more you can pay attention to the story instead of the individual words. And the more you pay attention to the story, the more you notice — about the characters, about the world the author created, about what the story is really about underneath the plot.

Your goal during this packet is to read every day — at least 20-30 minutes of independent reading in addition to any read-aloud time with your mentor. You will read complete books, not just chapters or excerpts. A whole book tells a whole story, and the experience of living inside a story for days or weeks — caring about what happens, wondering how it will end — is one of the great pleasures of being a reader.

### Part 2: Folk Tales and What They Teach

Folk tales are stories that were passed down by mouth for generations before anyone wrote them down. Every culture in the world has them. They are the stories ordinary people told each other — around fires, at bedtime, during long journeys. And because they were told by ordinary people to ordinary people, they carry the values, fears, and hopes of whole communities.

**Trickster tales** are found in nearly every culture. In West Africa and the African American tradition, the trickster is Anansi the Spider. In Native American traditions, the trickster is often Coyote or Raven. In Norse mythology, the trickster is Loki. Tricksters are clever, sometimes dishonest, always entertaining. They break rules, test boundaries, and sometimes get away with it — and sometimes do not. Trickster tales value cleverness, but they also show that cleverness without wisdom leads to trouble.

**Pourquoi tales** ("pourquoi" means "why" in French) are stories that explain why things are the way they are: why the leopard has spots, why the ocean is salty, why winter comes. These are not scientific explanations. They are imaginative explanations — and they reveal what a culture noticed and wondered about.

**Teaching tales** are stories told specifically to pass on wisdom. The Jataka tales from Buddhism, the parables from the Bible, and many African and Native American folk tales are teaching tales. They use story to make a point about how to live — and they do it so well that people have been retelling them for hundreds or thousands of years.

When you read folk tales, pay attention to: What does this culture think is important? What kind of hero does it admire? What behavior does it reward? What behavior does it punish?

### Part 3: Mythology — Stories About the Biggest Questions

You began hearing myths in A1. Now you will read them more deeply, either on your own or with your mentor, and you will start to compare: How do different mythologies answer the same big questions?

**Where did the world come from?** The Greeks said the world emerged from Chaos. The Norse said it was made from the body of a giant. Genesis says God created it in six days. Each answer tells you something about what that culture believed about the nature of the world and the power behind it.

**What makes a hero?** Greek heroes are often strong and brave, but also proud — and their pride often destroys them. Norse heroes know the world is doomed and fight anyway. Biblical heroes are often ordinary people chosen by God for extraordinary tasks — and they frequently resist. Each tradition's heroes tell you what that tradition valued most.

**Why do bad things happen?** The Greeks said the gods were capricious — they could be pleased or angered, and humans suffered when the gods were angry. The Norse said suffering was woven into the fabric of the universe. The Bible says suffering is connected to human choices, to sin, and to mystery. Each answer shapes an entire civilization's understanding of life.

You do not need to decide which mythology is "right." You need to notice what each one assumes about the world and how those assumptions shaped the people who believed them.

### Part 4: Literary Conversation — Talking About What You Read

Reading a book is only half the experience. The other half is talking about it with someone who cares. Literary conversation is not a book report. It is not a quiz. It is two people (or more) thinking together about what a story meant.

Good literary conversations include:
- **What happened** — retelling the story or part of it, including details that struck you
- **Why it matters** — what the story was really about, underneath the events
- **What you noticed** — details, patterns, or moments that caught your attention
- **What you felt** — honest emotional responses to the story
- **What you wondered** — questions the story raised that it did not answer
- **Whether you agree** — sometimes you will disagree with what a story seems to think is true, and that disagreement is worth exploring

The goal is not to arrive at the "correct" interpretation. It is to think more deeply about the story by sharing your thoughts with another person. You will often discover that someone else noticed something you missed, or that explaining your own interpretation helps you understand it better.

### Part 5: Comparing Stories Across Traditions

One of the most interesting things you will notice as you read folk tales and myths from around the world is how often different cultures tell similar stories. Nearly every culture has a flood story. Nearly every culture has a trickster. Nearly every culture has a story about a hero who goes on a journey and comes back changed.

These similarities tell you something important: human beings everywhere face the same basic questions — about where we come from, what matters, how to live with suffering, what makes a person good. We answer those questions differently, but we all ask them. And stories are how we ask.

The differences are equally interesting. A Greek hero might achieve glory through strength and cunning. A biblical hero might achieve it through faith and obedience. A Buddhist teaching tale might suggest that the greatest achievement is letting go of the desire for achievement altogether. Each tradition offers a different vision of what it means to live well — and hearing many visions gives you a richer understanding of what the possibilities are.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: Independent Reading [Ongoing — Backbone of the Packet]
The child reads independently for at least 20-30 minutes daily. After each reading session, the child narrates what they read — retelling the story and discussing what it meant. The mentor listens, asks questions, and engages in literary conversation. The narration after independent reading is the primary assessment.

### Study 2: Folk Tale Comparison [Early-to-Mid Packet]
Read trickster tales from at least three different cultures (e.g., Anansi from West Africa, Coyote from Native American tradition, Loki from Norse mythology). For each, the child narrates the story and identifies: What did the trickster do? Did it work? What does the story think about cleverness? Compare: How are these tricksters alike? How are they different? What does each culture seem to value?

### Study 3: Creation Story Comparison [Mid-Packet]
Read at least three creation stories from different traditions (e.g., Greek, Norse, biblical, Native American, African). For each, the child retells the story. Then discuss together: How does each story say the world began? What does each story think about the world — is it a good place, a dangerous place, a place of order or chaos? What is different about the way each culture imagines the beginning?

### Study 4: A Complete Novel — Discussion Series [Ongoing Through Packet]
The child reads a complete novel independently (or with minimal read-aloud support). Suggested: *The Lion, the Witch and the Wardrobe*, *The Hobbit*, or *Mrs. Frisby and the Rats of NIMH*. After every two or three chapters, hold a literary conversation. At the end of the book, discuss: What was the story really about? What did it value? What worldview does it present?

### Study 5: "Do You Agree with This Story?" [Late Packet]
Choose a story with a clear moral or worldview — *The Giving Tree*, a fairy tale where kindness is rewarded, an Aesop's fable, a myth where pride is punished. After reading and narrating, ask: Do you agree with what this story thinks? Is the story right about how the world works? Can you think of a time when the lesson of this story was true? Can you think of a time when it was not? This exercise builds the critical thinking that Weathering Storms develops fully.

### Study 6: Favorite Stories and Why [End of Packet]
The child selects their three favorite stories from the entire A1-A2 sequence and explains why each one matters to them. What did each story teach? What did each story make them feel? How are the three stories different from each other? This reflection consolidates the packet and reveals how the child's relationship with stories has developed.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Independent Reading Habit
**Developing well:** The child reads daily without being reminded. They choose books on their own. They talk about what they are reading with enthusiasm. They finish books.

**Developing slowly:** The child reads only when prompted. They do not finish books. Reading feels like a chore.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the material at the right level — challenging enough to be interesting, not so hard that reading is laborious? Does the child have access to a range of appealing books? Is there a protected reading time in the daily routine? Are screens competing with reading time?

### 2. Narration Quality After Independent Reading
**Developing well:** The child retells what they read with specific detail, sequence, and interpretation. They discuss what characters wanted and why things happened, not just what happened.

**Developing slowly:** Narrations are vague — "It was about a boy and some animals." No detail, no sequence, no interpretation.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the child actually comprehending what they read, or just turning pages? Test by reading a chapter together and comparing narrations. If the child narrates well after being read to but poorly after independent reading, the issue may be reading fluency rather than comprehension.

### 3. Cross-Cultural Awareness
**Developing well:** The child can name stories from multiple traditions and has begun to notice similarities and differences. They make connections spontaneously: "That story is like the one from Africa, except..."

**Developing slowly:** The child cannot name stories from different traditions or does not see connections between them.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the reading diet included enough diversity? Cross-cultural awareness requires cross-cultural reading. If the child has only read Western chapter books and not encountered folk tales from other traditions, the curriculum — not the child — needs adjustment.

### 4. Critical Engagement
**Developing well:** The child can articulate agreement or disagreement with a story's message. They can say what a story thinks is true and whether they agree. They ask questions like "But what if...?" or "That's not always true because..."

**Developing slowly:** The child accepts every story's message uncritically or cannot articulate what the story's message is.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you modeling critical engagement? Have you disagreed with a story in front of the child? Have you asked "Do you agree?" consistently and waited for a genuine answer rather than a compliant one?
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Rushing the Transition
A child who is not yet reading fluently enough for independent chapter books should not be pushed into A2. Continue A1 read-alouds while building reading fluency. A child forced to read independently before they are ready will associate reading with struggle rather than pleasure — the opposite of what this packet intends.

### Letting Independent Reading Replace Conversation
Independent reading is half the work. Conversation is the other half. A child who reads alone and never discusses what they read is developing the reading habit but not the interpretive habit. Make sure every reading session is followed by at least a brief conversation — even five minutes of "What happened? What did you think?" is enough to activate the child's analytical thinking.

### Limiting to One Tradition
If the child gravitates only toward fantasy novels and resists folk tales, you have a curricular problem to solve. Use folk tales as read-alouds or paired readings: "Before you go back to your chapter book, let's read this Anansi story together — it will take five minutes." Diverse exposure is a requirement, not an option.

### Over-Analyzing for the Child
At this age, the child is developing the habit of noticing, not the skill of rigorous analysis. If you find yourself lecturing about worldview and narrative structure, you have gone too far. Keep it conversational. Keep it at the child's level. The sophisticated analysis comes in Weathering Storms.
